title: Notes on conditional probability and Bayes' theorem
summary: Working through Module 2 of Course 3 in the Google Advanced Data Analytics certificate — what conditional probability and Bayes' theorem actually mean, and why they matter for data analysis.
body:
I just finished the probability module (Module 2, Course 3) of the Google Advanced Data Analytics certificate, and the two ideas that took the most effort to actually understand — not just memorize — were conditional probability and Bayes' theorem.

Conditional probability is the probability of an event happening given that another event has already happened, written as P(A|B). The part that tripped me up at first is that P(A|B) is not the same as P(B|A) — the probability someone has a disease given a positive test result is a completely different number from the probability of a positive test result given that someone has the disease, even though both involve the same two events. Mixing these up is apparently one of the most common reasoning errors people make with probability, and now I understand why: they sound interchangeable in plain English but aren't mathematically.

Bayes' theorem is the formula that lets you go from one conditional probability to the other:

P(A|B) = [P(B|A) × P(A)] / P(B)

What made this click for me was a medical testing example: even with a fairly accurate test, if the disease itself is rare, a positive result still might not mean you actually have it, because the number of false positives among the (much larger) healthy population can outweigh the true positives among the (much smaller) sick population. The 'base rate' — how common the condition is to begin with — matters just as much as the test's accuracy.

Why this matters for data analysis: any time you're interpreting a flag, an alert, or a classification model's output, you're implicitly working with conditional probability. A fraud-detection model with 99% accuracy can still produce mostly false alarms if fraud itself is rare, for the exact same base-rate reason as the medical test example. Understanding Bayes' theorem is what lets you ask the right follow-up question instead of just trusting the headline accuracy number.
